{{Short description|Fundamental physical constant that relates photon energy to its frequency}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Planck constant
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Photoelectric effect]], [[Black-body radiation]], [[Uncertainty principle]]
| symbol = h
| value = 6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s (exact by definition)
}}

The '''Planck constant''' <math>h</math> is a fundamental [[physical constant]] that is the quantum of electromagnetic action. It relates the [[energy]] of a [[photon]] to its [[frequency]]:

:<math>E = h\nu = \hbar\omega</math>

where <math>\nu</math> is the frequency and <math>\omega = 2\pi\nu</math> is the [[angular frequency]]. The constant was introduced by [[Max Planck]] in 1900 to explain [[black-body radiation]], marking the birth of [[quantum mechanics]].

Since 2019, the Planck constant has the exact defined value:
:<math>h = 6.62607015 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}</math>

== Reduced Planck constant ==
The [[reduced Planck constant]] (Dirac constant) <math>\hbar</math> (h-bar) is:
:<math>\hbar = \frac{h}{2\pi} = 1.054571817 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}</math>

<math>\hbar</math> appears naturally in most quantum mechanical equations.

== Fundamental roles ==

=== Energy–frequency relation ===
:<math>E = h\nu = \hbar\omega</math> — the [[Planck–Einstein relation]] for photons

=== De Broglie wavelength ===
{{Main|De Broglie wavelength}}
:<math>\lambda = \frac{h}{p}</math> — every particle has an associated wavelength

=== Heisenberg uncertainty principle ===
{{Main|Uncertainty principle}}
:<math>\Delta x \, \Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}</math>

=== Schrödinger equation ===
{{Main|Schrödinger equation}}
:<math>i\hbar\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\Psi = \hat{H}\Psi</math>

=== Commutation relations ===
:<math>[\hat{x}, \hat{p}] = i\hbar</math>

== Role in the SI redefinition ==
In the [[2019 redefinition of the SI base units]], the Planck constant was given a fixed exact value, which (together with definitions of the [[second]] and [[metre]]) defines the [[kilogram]].

== Historical significance ==
Planck's introduction of <math>h</math> to explain [[black-body radiation]] resolved the [[ultraviolet catastrophe]] of classical physics and inadvertently founded [[quantum theory]]. Planck himself initially viewed the constant as a mathematical convenience, but [[Albert Einstein]]'s explanation of the [[photoelectric effect]] (1905) confirmed its physical reality.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Photoelectric effect]]
* [[Quantum mechanics]]
* [[Uncertainty principle]]
* [[Wave–particle duality]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Physical constants]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Max Planck]]
